title: Looking at the frontispiece of 'A tale of a tub' by Jonathan Swift
author: Gemini (Modello Linguistico di Google)
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description:
The images have been created by Gemini with prompts given by A.C. Sparavigna. First, a screenshot of the frontispiece of "A tale of a tub", by Jonathan Swift, https://archive.org/details/bim_eighteenth-century_a-tale-of-a-tub-written_swift-jonathan_1727/page/n1/mode/2up is provided to Gemni, asking for a description, without  inromation about book and author. 
 Gemini tells: "Ahoy there! This looks like a dramatic scene unfolding on the high seas. I see a ship caught in a tumultuous storm, with large, crashing waves all around it. The ship itself appears to be listing quite heavily, its sail billowing in what must be strong winds. Adding to the danger, a large sea creature, perhaps a whale or a monstrous fish, is surfacing right next to the vessel. It looks quite menacing! There's also a barrel or some sort of cylindrical object being tossed about in the rough waters nearby. The sky above is dark and filled with stormy clouds, really emphasizing the perilous situation. It's quite the intense depiction!" 
 The conversation continues with A.C. Sparavigna providing the title of the book, the author, and the following  information about Swift's explanation for the title of the book: "the Ship of State was threatened by a whale (specifically, the Leviathan of Thomas Hobbes) and the new political societies (the Rota Club is mentioned). His book is intended to be a tub that the sailors of state (the nobles and ministers) might toss over the side to divert the attention of the beast (those who questioned the government and its right to rule). Hobbes was highly controversial in the Restoration, but Swift's invocation of Hobbes might well be ironic. The narrative of the brothers is a faulty allegory, and Swift's narrator is either a madman or a fool. The book is not one that could occupy the Leviathan, or preserve the Ship of State, so Swift may be intensifying the dangers of Hobbes's critique rather than allaying them to provoke a more rational response" (Wikipedia). 
 Sparavigna asks for an image with the following promt: "Image. Mythological ambient. Barocco style. A dramatic scene unfolding on the high seas. Show a ship caught in a tumultuous storm, with large, crashing waves all around it. The ship itself appears to be listing quite heavily, its sail billowing in what must be strong winds. Adding to the danger, a large sea creature, perhaps the Leviathan, is surfacing right next to the vessel.There's also a barrel or some sort of cylindrical object being tossed about in the rough waters nearby. The sky above is dark and filled with stormy clouds." 
 Three images have been prepared.
